description: Per-subsystem breakdown of BEAM runtime internals: process/port/atom saturation detail, memory segments, ETS, MSACC scheduler microstate, GC, I/O, memory allocator fragmentation, Erlang distribution link health, and Mnesia. Generic for any Elixir/Erlang project using prometheus_ex.
name: BEAM / Erlang VM - Deep Dive
title: BEAM / Erlang VM - Deep Dive

k8s_pod.description: Pod name filter — select specific pods to scope all panels to one application
k8s_pod.name: k8s_pod

widgets.description: Live processes (solid) vs hard limit (dashed). Gap = headroom. Shrinking gap = leak or load spike.
widgets.title: Process Count vs Limit

widgets.description: >85% is critical — BEAM refuses to spawn new processes at the limit.

filter.expression: deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod
deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod

widgets.title: Process Utilization %

widgets.description: Ports represent open sockets, files, and NIF handles. Exhaustion causes connection failures.

filter.expression: deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod
deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod

widgets.title: Port Count vs Limit

widgets.description: Port exhaustion does not crash the node but causes {error, system_limit} on new connections.

filter.expression: deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod
deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod

widgets.title: Port Utilization %

widgets.description: Atom table is global and never shrinks. Monotonic growth without restart = atom leak (dynamic atom creation).
widgets.title: Atom Count vs Limit

widgets.description: Memory allocated to all Erlang process stacks and heaps. Growing without a matching process count increase = per-process memory leak.
widgets.title: Process Heap Memory

widgets.description: BEAM VM system memory: ETS, code, stack, and VM overhead. Excludes process heap.
widgets.title: System Memory

widgets.description: Memory consumed by the atom table. Correlated with atom count.
widgets.title: Atom Memory

widgets.description: Off-heap reference-counted binaries. Steady monotonic growth between GCs = binary leak (processes holding large binary references).
widgets.title: Binary Memory (off-heap)

widgets.description: Total memory used by all ETS tables. Correlates with table count and row density.
widgets.title: ETS Memory

widgets.description: Memory occupied by loaded BEAM modules. Significant spikes after hot upgrades or repeated code:load calls.
widgets.title: Code Memory

widgets.description: Total open ETS tables. Steady growth without a corresponding application load increase = table leak (missing :ets.delete).
widgets.title: ETS Table Count

widgets.description: Hard ceiling for ETS tables (default 8192). Reference line — compare against ETS Count.
widgets.title: ETS Table Limit

widgets.description: ETS tables used vs limit. >80% warrants investigation into table ownership and lifecycle.

filter.expression: deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod
deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod

widgets.title: ETS Utilization %

widgets.description: Disk-backed ETS tables. Normally 0 in most apps. Unexpected growth = open disk tables not being closed.
widgets.title: DETS (Disk ETS) Table Count

widgets.description: Fraction of scheduler time each MSACC state consumes. 'aux' + 'check_io' + 'emulator' should dominate; high 'sleep' = idle schedulers; high 'gc' = GC pressure.
widgets.title: MSACC: aux / check_io / emulator / gc

widgets.description: Scheduler time in port / nif / bif / send. Elevated nif = heavy NIF calls blocking schedulers; elevated send = message-passing saturation.
widgets.title: MSACC: port / nif / bif / send

widgets.description: Scheduler time asleep. High sleep = under-utilised schedulers (normal during low load) or blocked waiting on I/O.
widgets.title: MSACC: sleep

widgets.description: Scheduler time in alloc / other / timers. Elevated alloc = heavy allocation pressure; elevated timers = many active timer references.
widgets.title: MSACC: alloc / other / timers

widgets.description: GC runs per second. Short spikes are normal; sustained high rates with rising heap = object churn.
widgets.title: GC Count Rate

widgets.description: Bytes reclaimed per second by GC. High reclaim with high GC count = frequent short-lived allocation churn.
widgets.title: GC Bytes Reclaimed Rate

widgets.description: Words moved across generations per GC cycle. Large word counts = major GC activity; correlate with pauses.
widgets.title: GC Words Reclaimed Rate

widgets.description: Raw bytes received by all ports (sockets, pipes). Sustained high read throughput is normal; sudden drops = upstream pressure.
widgets.title: I/O Bytes In Rate

widgets.description: Raw bytes sent by all ports. Sustained high write throughput is normal; compare with in-rate for asymmetry.
widgets.title: I/O Bytes Out Rate

widgets.description: Total context switches across all schedulers per second. Sudden step-changes correlate with process spawning bursts or supervisor restarts.
widgets.title: Context Switches Rate

widgets.description: Total reductions (function calls + tail calls) per second. Steady baseline; spikes indicate CPU-intensive work or hot loops.
widgets.title: Reductions Rate

widgets.description: Bytes allocated by the BEAM allocator vs bytes actually used by live data. Gap = fragmentation overhead.
widgets.title: Allocator MBCS Pool Block Size

widgets.description: Number of multi-block carriers in the pool. Growing without load growth = fragmented carrier accumulation.
widgets.title: Allocator MBCS Pool Block Count

widgets.description: Single-block carriers created by the allocator. SBC growth = large allocations exceeding MBC threshold; consider +MBcst tuning.
widgets.title: Allocator SBC Block Size

widgets.description: Fragmentation ratio = (allocated − used) / allocated. Values >40% sustained = fragmented memory; consider allocator flags or node restart.
widgets.title: MBC Fragmentation %

widgets.description: Allocated carrier size (total reserved) vs block size (live data). Wide gap = wasted carrier memory.
widgets.title: MBC Block vs Carrier Size

widgets.description: Distribution bytes sent per second to remote nodes. Sudden drop = node disconnect or network partition.
widgets.title: Distribution Bytes Sent Rate

widgets.description: Distribution bytes received per second from remote nodes. Spikes = large remote message deliveries or data transfers.
widgets.title: Distribution Bytes Received Rate

widgets.description: Messages sent across distribution links per second. High rate + growing queues = dist buffer saturation risk.
widgets.title: Distribution Messages Sent Rate

widgets.description: Messages received over distribution links per second. Asymmetry with sent rate = one-way traffic imbalance.
widgets.title: Distribution Messages Received Rate

widgets.description: Pending bytes in the distribution send buffer. Sustained non-zero values = congested distribution link.
widgets.title: Distribution Send Pending

widgets.description: Packets sent across distribution links. Low-frequency, high-byte packets = large message transfers.
widgets.title: Distribution Packets Sent Rate

widgets.description: Packets received over distribution links. Compare with sent rate and bytes for flow analysis.
widgets.title: Distribution Packets Received Rate

widgets.description: Active Mnesia transactions. Sustained high counts = long-running transactions blocking table locks.
widgets.title: Mnesia Held Locks

widgets.description: Processes waiting to acquire Mnesia locks. Non-zero = lock contention; high values = potential deadlock or slow transactions.
widgets.title: Mnesia Lock Queue Depth

widgets.description: Mnesia transaction restarts due to lock conflicts. Elevated restart rate = high write contention; consider table sharding or optimistic locking.
widgets.title: Mnesia Transaction Restart Rate

widgets.description: Mnesia transactions aborted per second. Aborts can be caused by conflicts, node failures, or application errors.
widgets.title: Mnesia Transaction Abort Rate

widgets.description: Mnesia transactions committed per second. Baseline for throughput; compare with abort and restart rates for health ratio.
widgets.title: Mnesia Transaction Commit Rate

widgets.description: Mnesia log entries written per second. Disk-based tables only. High log rate + low commit rate = replay overhead.
widgets.title: Mnesia Logged Transactions Rate

widgets.description: Failed Mnesia writes. Any non-zero value = data integrity issue or schema mismatch.
widgets.title: Mnesia Failed Transaction Rate

widgets.description: Mnesia tables currently open. Steady count = stable schema; sudden drop = node partition or table deletion.
widgets.title: Mnesia Memory Usage

widgets.description: Mnesia tables with unsynchronised data across nodes. Non-zero = split-brain risk. Should return to zero after healing.
widgets.title: Mnesia Unsynchronised Tables

widgets.description: Subscribed Mnesia event listeners. Unexpected drop = subscriber process crashed.
widgets.title: Mnesia Subscriptions

widgets.description: Number of connected Mnesia cluster nodes. Drop below expected count = node departure or partition.
widgets.title: Mnesia Running DB Nodes

widgets.description: Mnesia tables registered in the schema. Sudden change = schema migration or table deletion.
widgets.title: Mnesia Stopped DB Nodes

widgets.description: Table copies held by this node. Reduction = replica departure or table deletion.
widgets.title: Mnesia Held Locks (detail)

widgets.description: Mnesia event subscribers. Each type of activity subscription appears as a separate line.
widgets.title: Mnesia Subscriptions by Type

widgets.description: Mnesia commit rate vs abort rate vs restart rate. High abort-to-commit ratio = significant write contention.
widgets.title: Mnesia Transaction Health Overview

widgets.description: Memory consumed by Mnesia data vs process heap vs system. Helps identify if Mnesia dominates memory.
widgets.title: Mnesia vs VM Memory

widgets.description: Mnesia tables as currently reported. Any drops = partition or node failure.
widgets.title: Mnesia Node Status

widgets.description: Mnesia tables with unsynchronised copies (split-brain indicator) vs held locks (contention indicator) over time.
widgets.title: Mnesia Unsync Tables vs Held Locks

widgets.description: Active Mnesia transaction coordinators (dirty vs transactional). Dominance of dirty writes = performance but no isolation.
widgets.title: Mnesia Active Transaction Coordinators

widgets.description: % of atom table used. Atom table never shrinks — runaway growth = leak.

filter.expression: deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod
deployment.environment in $environment AND k8s.cluster.name in $k8s_cluster AND k8s.pod.name in $k8s_pod

widgets.title: Atom Utilization %
